answers:
Certinia ERP Cloud seemlessly integrates with SalesForce and Certinia's PSA platform, which makes running a consulting services business much faster than when we had a siloed tech stack. Many of the accounting features (especially revenue recognition) make bookkeeping and reporting much easier than with other systems we've used.

I like the fact that Certinia has a flexible, customizable interface.

The customer support with Certinia is overall really good. It's easy to get to know the people who work in the company, and they genuinely care about your success.

There are 2 issues with Certinia ERP Cloud that, if resolved, will make Certinia a much better product.

The first isssue is that the GUI needs work. There's a lack of consistency in the look and feel of many of the different areas of the accounting, which requires me as the end user to do a lot of pattern-switching when using the program, which slows me down. Additionally, the system is not easy to use out of the box and almost always requires a ton of customization to bring to a workable, stable state, which adds considerable implementation time and learning curves to organizations who aren't ready to roll up their sleaves and dive into a lot of technical issues. If you're a company that prefers the ease of use of a what-you-see-is-what-you-get program like QuickBooks or NetSuite, Certinia ERP Cloud may not be for you.

The second issue is that it's very difficult to find technical documentation, help files, and training videos for Certinia ERP Cloud, which adds unecessary difficult to a product that already has a high learning curve. I had to do a lot of training sessions with Certinia technical customer success managers to learn the skills necessary to run my accounting department with Certinia when I would have preferred a more self-paced learning program.

The biggest benefit to using Certinia ERP Cloud is having a single-sign-on solution to manage our entire sales to cash pipeline. Having everything on the Force.com platform really speeds up our workflows and provides a single source of truth for our metrics.

title: ERP on a daily work Basis

answers:
FF's Accounting package (FFA) is hosted on the Salesforce platform, allowing a lot of flexibility and customizations to meet business needs. That said, the product comes out-of-the-box with all the primary functionality most businesses would require. They are also making inroads with a lot of the weaker areas of the product, and the roadmap in these areas is reasonably strong.

However, the strongest area of FFA is when you partner it with the rest of the Financial Force solution. In our case, we also use PSA and the fairly seamless integration between the 2 products is a huge win.

We are based in Australia and the localisation of the product is currently fairly limited. It is on the roadmap, but basic functionality like bank feeds and BAS reports do not exist, which can be a difficult discussion with stakeholders when put aside a lot of other products that offer this out-of-the-box.

With the flexibility of the system comes a learning curve. Users have to be prepared to learn the ins and outs of the system, and how the data model fundamentally works, to get the most out of it. You will need a technical system administrator who has the time to dedicate to managing the product.

The user interface can sometimes be clumsy and confusing, or it can take several clicks to do basic tasks. I believe this will improve over time but is a current limitation.

Through the FF suite in general, combined with Salesforce, we are able to provide a end-to-end business solution on a single platform which is a huge efficiency gain for technology and continuous improvement much easier.

title: ErP for it industry

answers: System interface is nice and navigating between linked objects is mostly easy and intuitive. I also like the flexibility to build many different custom reports.

My #1 complaint and the biggest pain point for my team is that the "As of Aging" report (AP/AR aging) cannot be configured to run based on financial period, only document date (i.e. the vendor's invoice date), so for our purposes the output is basically worthless. This has caused dozens, if not hundreds, of hours of additional work for my team in order to provide something workable to our auditors, and we have lost visibility into true discrepancies between our AP subledger and our GL because we instead spend hours trying to reconcile timing differences that are not really variances. 

Check printing is also incredibly inefficient because our company uses blank check stock and we had to implement Conga software since FFA itself does not allow printing on blank check stock. The process itself is very slow and inefficient since Conga is really a mail merge software and not designed for check printing. I wish FF and our consultants had been upfront about these limitations before selling us on using Conga because if we had known about this ahead of time, we might have made a business decision to switch to preprinted check stock. Furthermore, FFA does not allow printing a check without first creating an invoice, so in cases where we wouldn't normally have an invoice to cut a check, we are forced to create an invoice, unapply it from the check after printing the check, and credit out the invoice, which is time-consuming and clogs up our GL. 

Another complaint is the low line limits on various documents, such as journal entries, purchase requisitions, payable invoices, etc. The fact that we need to break some of these items into multiple documents creates a lot of unnecessary work for my team, and we don't understand why there are such low line limitations (e.g. 80 lines for invoices).
Better tracking of project costs and integration with the financial module compared with our previous system

Really investigate the limitations of the system before selecting it. It may work well for other companies, but we have had serious issues and inefficiencies with the aging reports, check printing and line limitations.

title: Consolidation Made Easy

answers:
My favorite quality of FinancialForce is the seamless integration with Salesforce.com. The platform operates much in the same way as Salesforce, so customizing and reporting were easy and quick to learn. This made the integration process go much smoother and I was able to train my team quickly on using the new financial system. Throughout my history of using FinancialForce, whenever I did encounter a questions or problem, opening a case was easy and the response time was within 1-2 days. 
Another key functionality that I really enjoy with having both our operations and finances under one login, is the reduced amount of data entry. We have greatly reduced the amount of time spent on duplicate data entry by operating in the same system. FinancialForce also offers a lot of automated functionality, but one we use is the automated cash matching, which takes out another step of manual data entry. The operating efficiencies we've leveraged by implementing FinancialForce have paid for the cost and then some.

We are a small organization of 20 and we do not have a developer in-house. We struggled with the FinancialForce reporting app, as this requires some use of code and developer 'speak'. The standard Salesforce reporting allows for FinancialForce data as well, so we rely on this function more often, but when it comes to the standard P&L structure for our board, we needed a 'pretty' report, and this requires the FinancialForce reporting app. During our implementation we were promised by the implementation partner that they would write the reports for us, however this was never delivered. FinancialForce is working on rectifying the problem for us, but it will require some additional hours and financial resources on our end.

By implementing FinancialForce into our operations, we solved the problem of operating out of two separate systems. This required tedious and time consuming monthly reconciliations between our CRM and our financials. We eliminated the double data entry, and now we touch the data once. We are also able to increase the user adoption and visibility of our financial data across the organization. 
For my organization, about 90% of our operations are based out of Salesforce.com, so the choice to implement FinancialForce was almost a no-brainer. We are also a non-profit, so we always have to look at the cost, but again, the cost of implementing FinancialForce was not only competitive with other financial software, but the savings of operating efficiencies played into our decision making. A seamless implementation onto a new platform was key for reducing the impact on day-to-day operations.
 Better
The integration process was timely and costly, but you really need to invest upfront to ensure the platform operates the way you need it to for the long haul.

title: Administrator

answers:
Bryce Group is a holding company, which focus on commodities trading on global markets. We are expanding the geography of our operations and the types of commodities we trade involving increasing volumes. FinancialForce Accounting has given us a real-time view of financials and has helped us streamline and create consistent processes to allow us to scale and focus on other important aspects of running the business. 
The FF Accounting allow us to customise and add additional functionality to the system. Our IT team creates additional applications such as contracts, logistics that allow us achieve customization of our business process. We create "bank bridges", which automatically generates cash entries using banking software "bank-client". We also redesign the standard invoices using our own stiles.

There is one issue we cannot solve so far - we need quantity reported in invoices and Noted with three decimal digits as our quantity is in ton and price calculated by kilogram. Hope that this issue will be solved soon.
Additionally, we are expecting more from FinancialForce platform for reporting to customise our Financial Statements reports and MI reports.

We implemented FinancialForce at the beginning of 2013 and achieved efficiency in producing MI, transparency into financials across the business that helped us identify an unexpected source of costs and reduce administrative costs. Our data now consistent and reliable, which has helped, elevate credibility with banks. FinancialForce Accounting allows each company in Bryce Group to see exact P&L on any account so they can quickly respond if see any problems e.g. restructure deals, move funds around. As an open book trading company being able to share financial reports with shareholders/clients is a huge advantage. FinancialForce

name: James F.
title: A great add on for a powerful CRM

answers: Since app is built on the Salesforce force.com platform, all accounting functions can be integrated with any standard or custom Salesforce object. This basically "opens the doors" between sales and/or operations to the accounting department and allows for sharing and collaborating around key metrics and financial data. FinancialForce also provides great customer support in terms of turnaround time once an issue is logged and in quality of responses.

The more robust reporting (for specific types of financial reports) has a steep learning curve and previously was not exportable to any other format (i.e. csv, xlsx,). The company is dedicated to improving and rolling out enhanced reporting each release however, so have seen major progress made in this area.

As a caveat, I'd like to point out that I loved this product so much as a user (and internally implemented it for two companies for whom I worked as an employee) that I became an external implementation consultant for FinancialForce. That said, I still counsel companies looking to implement FinancialForce accounting that they really need to understand their current accounting processes, come up with a defined list of requirements - particularly where the "marriage" of Salesforce standard or custom objects and FinancialForce accounting objects is concerned - and understand the level of commitment to the cloud that the company and the accounting department is making. Accounting folks are notoriously conservative, skeptical and change-averse, so cloud-based anything (but especially accounting systems) often do not resonate with them. But they are practical, and will ultimately realize the decreased TCO, ROI and mobility benefits that cloud accounting applications offer. Using Salesforce? FinancialForce
name: Lynne T.
title: Accounting is a "no-brainer"

answers: Built on the Force.com platform you can code your processes around FinancialForce using their APIs or directly manipulating their database so gives you a lot of flexibility on how you develop around FinancialForce. Has a nice customer community portal where you can share ideas, log issues etc. Support is great and if anything does crop up as an issue they really work hard to get it fixed.

It was one of the first cloud based accountancy packages and with that has some architectural decisions that I think they realised was a mistake now but with every release they fix these. Invoices need to be hand coded in Apex VisualForce, there is no "Invoice creation wizard", but FinancialForce are happy to do this for you. When you create an invoice from an opportunity FinancialForce assumes that you want to invoice every line on the opportunity. This isn't the case sometimes, you can delete the lines off the invoice but then it becomes hard to see which lines have been invoiced and which haven't. You can't invoice multiple opportunities at one time. But saying this we have resolved them all by using custom apex code. Trying to fit FinancialForce into a developer org is tricky due to the amount of configuration data that is required.

Watch out if you need to use Reporting Balances it does use a lot of storage data in Salesforce. Make sure you know your financial processes and the reports that you need to create from the data and you these these. If you can a good idea is to do a parallel run using your existing finance system and FinancialForce to iron out any kinks.
